content.text:
Design a Parking Lot System
MEDIUM
role
Software Development Engineer 1
Round 4
Coding Round 2: Low-Level Design
Low-Level Design (LLD)
exp
#1789 · Apr 2026
○ no offer
System Design
Low-Level Design (LLD)
Object Oriented Design (OOD)
Object-Oriented Programming
OOPS
Clean Coding
SOLID

▸
View problem statement & approach
Problem statement
Design a system to manage a multi level parking lot.

The system should support the following requirements:

The parking lot has multiple floors, with each floor having rows of parking spots.
It can accommodate various vehicle types, such as motorcycles, cars, and buses.
Parking spots come in different sizes (e.g., small, medium, large). A large vehicle might require multiple adjacent spots.
The system must be able to find and assign the first available spot suitable for a vehicle upon its arrival.
A ticket should be generated upon entry, containing unique details like vehicle information, spot number, and entry time.
Upon exit, the system must calculate the parking fee based on the duration of the stay and process the payment.
Display boards at the entrance should show real time counts of available spots for each vehicle type.
Describe the classes, data models, and core algorithms you would use. Also, discuss how you would handle scalability and concurrency for a busy parking lot with many vehicles entering and exiting simultaneously.

Candidate's approach
I outlined the UML flow and defined the core classes for the system. However, I struggled to complete the full working code within the time limit as I hadn't prepared extensively for low-level design.
I'll build and verify a complete, runnable design before presenting it. Let me write the code first.

content.content:
[exit code: 0]
--- stdout ---
initial capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 1}
duplicate rejected: vehicle CAR1 already parked
bus capacity now: 0
no spot rejected: No spot available for BUS
unknown ticket rejected: unknown ticket NOPE
car 2h fee: 40
after release capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 0}
concurrency: parked=2 rejected=8 (exactly 2 spots)
ALL ASSERTIONS PASSED
--- stderr ---
(empty)

content.text:
All assertions pass. Here is the complete, interview-ready design.

## 1. Requirements & Clarifications

**Problem:** Design an OO system to manage a **multi-level parking lot** that assigns spots to vehicles on entry, issues tickets, computes fees on exit, processes payment, and shows live availability — all safely under concurrent entry/exit.

**Functional requirements**
- Multiple floors → rows → spots; spots have sizes (small/medium/large).
- Vehicle types: motorcycle, car, bus. A bus needs **multiple adjacent** large spots.
- Find & assign the **first available** suitable spot(s) on arrival.
- Issue a **ticket** (id, vehicle, spot(s), entry time).
- On exit: compute fee from duration, process payment, free the spot(s).
- **Display boards** show real-time free counts per vehicle type.

**Clarifying questions to ask**
- Can a smaller vehicle take a larger spot if its size is full? (I'll assume **strict size match** to keep it deterministic — easy to relax.)
- "Adjacent" = same row, contiguous columns? (Assuming yes.)
- Pricing model — flat hourly, or tiered/day rates? (Assuming hourly per type, behind a Strategy so it swaps freely.)
- Single physical lot (in-memory) or distributed across DB? (Assuming single-process; I'll discuss scaling.)
- Multiple entry/exit gates parking concurrently? (**Yes** — drives the concurrency model.)

> 💬 *"Before I code, let me lock scope: I'll assume strict spot-size matching, a bus takes 3 adjacent large spots, pricing is hourly behind a strategy interface, and multiple gates operate concurrently so thread-safety matters. Shout if you'd like different assumptions."*

## 2. Use Cases & Actors

| Actor | Use case |
|---|---|
| Driver | Enter (get ticket), exit (pay), leave |
| Entry gate | Request spot, issue ticket |
| Exit gate | Compute fee, take payment, release spot |
| Display board | Show live free counts per type |
| Admin | Add floors/spots, set pricing |

Core interactions: **park(vehicle) → ticket**, **exit(ticket) → fee**, **pay_and_release(ticket)**, and **board.update()** fired on every state change.

## 3. Core Entities (Objects)

- **Vehicle** — plate + type.
- **ParkingSpot** — id, size, location (floor/row/col), occupancy.
- **ParkingFloor** — owns rows of spots; knows how to find contiguous free spots.
- **Ticket** — entry record: vehicle, assigned spots, times, fee, paid flag.
- **FeeStrategy** — pluggable fee calculation.
- **DisplayBoard** — observer that renders live counts.
- **ParkingLot** — the **orchestrator**: registry of active tickets, assignment, exit, payment, notifications.

> 💬 *"I extract the nouns from the prompt — lot, floor, spot, vehicle, ticket, fee, display — give each a single responsibility, then make ParkingLot the orchestrator that owns the truth about every in-flight ticket."*

## 4. Class Design (diagram)

This shows the class structure and relationships.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ParkingLot {
        -floors
        -active_tickets
        -plate_to_ticket
        -boards
        -lock
        +park(vehicle) Ticket
        +exit_vehicle(id) float
        +pay_and_release(id) bool
    }
    class ParkingFloor {
        -rows
        +find_contiguous(size, n)
    }
    class ParkingSpot {
        -size
        -occupied_by
        +is_free
    }
    class Vehicle {
        -plate
        -vtype
    }
    class Ticket {
        -vehicle
        -spots
        -entry_time
        -fee
        -paid
    }
    class FeeStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +calculate(ticket, exit) float
    }
    class HourlyFeeStrategy
    class DisplayBoard {
        +update(counts)
    }
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" ParkingFloor
    ParkingFloor "1" o-- "*" ParkingSpot
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" Ticket
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" DisplayBoard
    ParkingLot --> FeeStrategy
    FeeStrategy <|.. HourlyFeeStrategy
    Ticket --> Vehicle
    Ticket --> ParkingSpot
```

**Relationships:** ParkingLot **composes** floors, which compose spots (lifecycle owned). ParkingLot **holds** the active-ticket registry and the boards, and **depends on** a FeeStrategy abstraction (not the concrete class). A Ticket **references** a Vehicle and its assigned spots.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

- **Strategy** — `FeeStrategy` lets pricing change (hourly, flat, weekend) without touching the lot. ✅ wired in code.
- **Observer** — `DisplayBoard`s register with the lot; every park/release calls `_notify()` to push fresh counts. ✅ wired.
- **Singleton** — one physical lot → `ParkingLot.get_instance()` (double-checked lock). ✅ wired.
- **Factory-ish spec table** — `VEHICLE_SPEC` maps type → (size, count) so adding a vehicle type is one line.

**SOLID:** *SRP* — each class has one job; *OCP* — new fee rules / vehicle types without editing the orchestrator; *DIP* — lot depends on the `FeeStrategy` interface, not a concrete.

> 💬 **Layman:** *"Strategy = the price calculator is a swappable cartridge. Observer = the display boards are subscribers that get auto-pinged whenever a spot changes. Singleton = there's exactly one lot object everyone shares."*

## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class

**Enums + spec table** — the single source of truth for what each vehicle needs. A bus needs **3 adjacent large** spots; this table makes adding a type trivial (OCP).

```python
class VehicleType(Enum):
    MOTORCYCLE = "MOTORCYCLE"; CAR = "CAR"; BUS = "BUS"

class SpotSize(Enum):
    SMALL = 1; MEDIUM = 2; LARGE = 3

VEHICLE_SPEC = {
    VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE: (SpotSize.SMALL, 1),
    VehicleType.CAR:        (SpotSize.MEDIUM, 1),
    VehicleType.BUS:        (SpotSize.LARGE, 3),
}
```

**ParkingSpot** — knows its size/location and who occupies it. `occupied_by` holds a plate (not a bool) so we never lose *which* vehicle is there.

```python
class ParkingSpot:
    def __init__(self, spot_id, size, floor, row, col):
        self.spot_id, self.size = spot_id, size
        self.floor, self.row, self.col = floor, row, col
        self.occupied_by = None
    @property
    def is_free(self):
        return self.occupied_by is None
```

**ParkingFloor** — owns rows and implements the **first-fit contiguous search**. Spots are kept column-sorted so "adjacent" is well defined; the run resets whenever a spot is busy or wrong-sized.

```python
class ParkingFloor:
    def __init__(self, floor_no):
        self.floor_no, self.rows = floor_no, {}
    def add_spot(self, spot):
        self.rows.setdefault(spot.row, []).append(spot)
        self.rows[spot.row].sort(key=lambda s: s.col)
    def find_contiguous(self, size, n):
        for row in sorted(self.rows):
            run = []
            for s in self.rows[row]:
                if s.is_free and s.size == size:
                    run.append(s)
                    if len(run) == n: return run
                else:
                    run = []
        return None
```

**FeeStrategy** — abstract; `HourlyFeeStrategy` rounds up to the next hour (min 1h) per type.

```python
class FeeStrategy(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def calculate(self, ticket, exit_time): ...

class HourlyFeeStrategy(FeeStrategy):
    RATE = {VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE:10, VehicleType.CAR:20, VehicleType.BUS:50}
    def calculate(self, ticket, exit_time):
        import math
        hours = max(1, math.ceil((exit_time - ticket.entry_time)/3600.0))
        return hours * self.RATE[ticket.vehicle.vtype]
```

**ParkingLot (orchestrator)** — owns the active-ticket registry, the plate index (blocks duplicate parking), the boards, and **one RLock** guarding all shared state. Note the **check-then-act inside the lock** in `park`: find spots *and* mark them occupied atomically, so two gates can't grab the same spot. Every outcome is explicit — a ticket, or a raised error; nothing is silently dropped.

```python
def park(self, vehicle):
    size, n = VEHICLE_SPEC[vehicle.vtype]
    with self._lock:
        if vehicle.plate in self.plate_to_ticket:
            raise ValueError(f"vehicle {vehicle.plate} already parked")
        for floor in self.floors:
            spots = floor.find_contiguous(size, n)
            if spots:
                for s in spots: s.occupied_by = vehicle.plate
                ticket = Ticket(vehicle, spots, time.time())
                self.active_tickets[ticket.ticket_id] = ticket
                self.plate_to_ticket[vehicle.plate] = ticket.ticket_id
                self._notify()
                return ticket
        raise RuntimeError(f"No spot available for {vehicle.vtype.value}")
```

Exit is split into **compute fee** (`exit_vehicle`) and **pay + free** (`pay_and_release`) so payment failure can't strand spots in a half state. Both validate the ticket id at the boundary.

## 7. Putting It Together (verified run)

I wrote the full program to `parking.py` and ran it. The driver exercises the tricky cases: normal park, **duplicate rejection**, bus needing 3 adjacent spots, **exhaustion (no spot)**, **unknown ticket**, fee math, release, and a **10-thread race for 2 spots**.

Actual output:

```text
initial capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 1}
duplicate rejected: vehicle CAR1 already parked
bus capacity now: 0
no spot rejected: No spot available for BUS
unknown ticket rejected: unknown ticket NOPE
car 2h fee: 40
after release capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 0}
concurrency: parked=2 rejected=8 (exactly 2 spots)
ALL ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

The concurrency line is the key proof: 10 motorcycles raced for 2 small spots and **exactly 2** got in — no double-booking, no lost request (the other 8 got a clean rejection).

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

End-to-end entry → exit for one car.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant L as ParkingLot
    participant F as Floor
    participant B as DisplayBoard
    D->>L: park(car)
    L->>F: find_contiguous(MEDIUM, 1)
    F-->>L: spot
    L->>L: mark occupied, create Ticket
    L->>B: update(counts)
    L-->>D: Ticket
    D->>L: exit_vehicle(ticket)
    L-->>D: fee
    D->>L: pay_and_release(ticket)
    L->>L: free spots, drop from registry
    L->>B: update(counts)
    L-->>D: released
```

The lot looks up a contiguous spot, commits the assignment under lock, registers the ticket, and pushes new counts to boards. On exit, fee is computed, then payment frees the spots and re-notifies boards.

## 9. Concurrency, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**Concurrency (one model, applied consistently)**
- Single **RLock** on `ParkingLot` guards *all* reads and writes of shared state (spots, registry, plate index, boards). Reentrant because `park`/`pay_and_release` call `_notify()` which also reads shared state.
- **Check-then-act is atomic**: find-and-occupy happens in the same critical section — the root fix for double-booking.

> 💬 **Layman:** *"Picking a spot and stamping your plate on it is one indivisible step behind a single lock, so two cars can never be told the same spot is free."*

**Edge cases handled:** unknown ticket id, duplicate vehicle, full/exhausted lot (explicit reject with reason), bus needing 3 adjacent spots that aren't available, fee minimum of 1 hour, spots correctly freed back into capacity after release.

**Extensibility without rewrites**
- New pricing → new `FeeStrategy`. New vehicle type → one line in `VEHICLE_SPEC`. New display/notification → another Observer.
- **Allow smaller-vehicle-in-bigger-spot** → make `find_contiguous` accept `size >= needed` with a fallback order.
- **Scaling a busy lot**: shard the lock **per floor** (or per entry gate) so floors don't contend; keep per-floor free-spot counts in atomic counters for O(1) board reads; persist tickets to a DB with the spot row versioned (optimistic locking) for multi-node deployments; entry gates can reserve from a per-floor free-spot queue instead of scanning.

> 💬 **30-second summary:** *"Multi-level lot composed of floors and sized spots. ParkingLot is the singleton orchestrator that owns the active-ticket registry. On entry it does a first-fit contiguous search — a bus grabs 3 adjacent large spots — and the find-and-occupy is atomic under one lock, so concurrent gates never double-book. Tickets capture entry time; on exit a swappable FeeStrategy prices the stay, payment frees the spots, and Observer display boards get live counts on every change. Every request ends in a ticket or an explicit rejection — nothing is silently dropped. I verified it, including a 10-thread race for 2 spots that admitted exactly 2."*

All assertions pass. Here is the complete, interview-ready design.

## 1. Requirements & Clarifications

**Problem:** Design an OO system to manage a **multi-level parking lot** that assigns spots to vehicles on entry, issues tickets, computes fees on exit, processes payment, and shows live availability — all safely under concurrent entry/exit.

- Multiple floors → rows → spots; spots have sizes (small/medium/large).
- Vehicle types: motorcycle, car, bus. A bus needs **multiple adjacent** large spots.

- Issue a **ticket** (id, vehicle, spot(s), entry time).
- On exit: compute fee from duration, process payment, free the spot(s).
- **Display boards** show real-time free counts per vehicle type.

- Single physical lot (in-memory) or distributed across DB? (Assuming single-process; I'll discuss scaling.)
- Multiple entry/exit gates parking concurrently? (**Yes** — drives the concurrency model.)

> 💬 *"Before I code, let me lock scope: I'll assume strict spot-size matching, a bus takes 3 adjacent large spots, pricing is hourly behind a strategy interface, and multiple gates operate concurrently so thread-safety matters. Shout if you'd like different assumptions."*

## 2. Use Cases & Actors

| Actor | Use case |

| Driver | Enter (get ticket), exit (pay), leave |
| Entry gate | Request spot, issue ticket |
| Exit gate | Compute fee, take payment, release spot |

```mermaid

        -floors
        -active_tickets
        -plate_to_ticket
        -boards
        -lock
        +park(vehicle) Ticket
        +exit_vehicle(id) float
        +pay_and_release(id) bool
    }

        -rows
        +find_contiguous(size, n)
    }
    class ParkingSpot {
        -size
        -occupied_by
        +is_free
    }
    class Vehicle {
        -plate
        -vtype
    }
    class Ticket {
        -vehicle
        -spots
        -entry_time
        -fee
        -paid
    }
    class FeeStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +calculate(ticket, exit) float
    }
    class HourlyFeeStrategy
    class DisplayBoard {
        +update(counts)
    }
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" ParkingFloor
    ParkingFloor "1" o-- "*" ParkingSpot
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" Ticket
    ParkingLot "1" o-- "*" DisplayBoard
    ParkingLot --> FeeStrategy
    FeeStrategy <|.. HourlyFeeStrategy
    Ticket --> Vehicle
    Ticket --> ParkingSpot
```

```python
    VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE: (SpotSize.SMALL, 1),
```

```python

    def calculate(self, ticket, exit_time): ...

    RATE = {VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE:10, VehicleType.CAR:20, VehicleType.BUS:50}
```

I wrote the full program to `parking.py` and ran it. The driver exercises the tricky cases: normal park, **duplicate rejection**, bus needing 3 adjacent spots, **exhaustion (no spot)**, **unknown ticket**, fee math, release, and a **10-thread race for 2 spots**.

Actual output:

```text
initial capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 1}
duplicate rejected: vehicle CAR1 already parked
bus capacity now: 0
no spot rejected: No spot available for BUS
unknown ticket rejected: unknown ticket NOPE
car 2h fee: 40
after release capacity: {'MOTORCYCLE': 2, 'CAR': 2, 'BUS': 0}
concurrency: parked=2 rejected=8 (exactly 2 spots)
ALL ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

The concurrency line is the key proof: 10 motorcycles raced for 2 small spots and **exactly 2** got in — no double-booking, no lost request (the other 8 got a clean rejection).

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

End-to-end entry → exit for one car.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant L as ParkingLot
    participant F as Floor
    participant B as DisplayBoard
    D->>L: park(car)
    L->>F: find_contiguous(MEDIUM, 1)
    F-->>L: spot
    L->>L: mark occupied, create Ticket
    L->>B: update(counts)
    L-->>D: Ticket
    D->>L: exit_vehicle(ticket)
    L-->>D: fee
    D->>L: pay_and_release(ticket)
    L->>L: free spots, drop from registry
    L->>B: update(counts)
    L-->>D: released
```

The lot looks up a contiguous spot, commits the assignment under lock, registers the ticket, and pushes new counts to boards. On exit, fee is computed, then payment frees the spots and re-notifies boards.

## 9. Concurrency, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**Concurrency (one model, applied consistently)**
- Single **RLock** on `ParkingLot` guards *all* reads and writes of shared state (spots, registry, plate index, boards). Reentrant because `park`/`pay_and_release` call `_notify()` which also reads shared state.
- **Check-then-act is atomic**: find-and-occupy happens in the same critical section — the root fix for double-booking.

> 💬 **Layman:** *"Picking a spot and stamping your plate on it is one indivisible step behind a single lock, so two cars can never be told the same spot is free."*

**Edge cases handled:** unknown ticket id, duplicate vehicle, full/exhausted lot (explicit reject with reason), bus needing 3 adjacent spots that aren't available, fee minimum of 1 hour, spots correctly freed back into capacity after release.

**Extensibility without rewrites**
- New pricing → new `FeeStrategy`. New vehicle type → one line in `VEHICLE_SPEC`. New display/notification → another Observer.
- **Allow smaller-vehicle-in-bigger-spot** → make `find_contiguous` accept `size >= needed` with a fallback order.
- **Scaling a busy lot**: shard the lock **per floor** (or per entry gate) so floors don't contend; keep per-floor free-spot counts in atomic counters for O(1) board reads; persist tickets to a DB with the spot row versioned (optimistic locking) for multi-node deployments; entry gates can reserve from a per-floor free-spot queue instead of scanning.

> 💬 **30-second summary:** *"Multi-level lot composed of floors and sized spots. ParkingLot is the singleton orchestrator that owns the active-ticket registry. On entry it does a first-fit contiguous search — a bus grabs 3 adjacent large spots — and the find-and-occupy is atomic under one lock, so concurrent gates never double-book. Tickets capture entry time; on exit a swappable FeeStrategy prices the stay, payment frees the spots, and Observer display boards get live counts on every change. Every request ends in a ticket or an explicit rejection — nothing is silently dropped. I verified it, including a 10-thread race for 2 spots that admitted exactly 2."*
